title: Nocturnal heart palpitations — causes and next steps
summary: Likely sleep apnea or SVT — book a GP this week for an ECG. Not an emergency unless you get chest pain or feel faint.
body:
What you described — heart racing during sleep, possibly ~120 bpm — is worth taking seriously but almost certainly not dangerous.

Most likely causes:

1. Sleep apnea — oxygen drops during apneas trigger an adrenaline spike → heart races. Classic pattern. More likely if you snore, wake gasping, or feel unrested in the mornings. Heart rate 'over 2Hz' fits this perfectly.

2. SVT (supraventricular tachycardia) — sudden fast heart rate, often benign, self-resolving. Very common. Usually starts and stops abruptly.

3. Nocturnal anxiety or stress response — can cause the same symptoms.

4. Caffeine / alcohol late in the day — lowers the threshold for both.

Less likely but worth ruling out: atrial fibrillation (AF) — more common 40+, irregular rather than fast-but-regular.

What to do:
- Book a GP appointment this week — ask for an ECG. Non-urgent but don't leave it months.
- If it happens again: count beats for 15s × 4 to get bpm; note if it stopped suddenly (SVT) or tapered off (more likely apnea/anxiety).
- Short term: cut caffeine after 2pm, no alcohol close to bed, try sleeping on your side.

If you get chest pain, shortness of breath, or feel faint during an episode — A&E, don't wait.
